paragraphs.text:
What these numbers suggest
- Hg(II) forms very strong 1:1 complexes with multidentate aminocarboxylates in dilute media (logK ≈ 18–19), and those ML adducts can further transform with OH−/H+ (logK ≈ 7 for hydroxo conversion; ≈5 for protonation), indicating pH-sensitive speciation around the chelated core.
- Hg(I) is dominated by solid Hg2Cl2 in chloride media; the strongly negative dissolution logK for [M2L2(s)] ⇌ [M]^2 + [L]^2 reflects the sparing solubility and limited aqueous Hg+ stability. In contrast, modest complexation with organic anions (e.g., chloroacetate) appears at high ionic strength.
- Organomercury cations show ligand-specific behavior: hydroxo binding is strong for PhHg+ (logK ~9.75), and chloride binds EtHg+ appreciably in saline conditions (logK 5.32). For MeHg+, the entry here provides thermodynamics (ΔS) but no logK for MeHgOH at 25 °C, I = 0.10 M.

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: Filler span: no useful database-groundable information.
Hg2+ ML logK values near 18–19: 17.73 and 19.3 at low ionic strength (≈0 M).
Hydroxo conversion constants: | logK | 7.4 | and | logK | 7.2 | for [ML]+[OH]→[M(OH)L].
Protonation of ML: | beta_def_788 | ``[ML] + [H] <=> [MHL]`` | logK | 5.2 |
Both [ML]+H and [ML]+OH equilibria exist (logK 5.2, 7.4), consistent with pH-dependent speciation.
Hg+ + Cl−: | beta_def_458 | ``[M2L2(s)] <=> [M]^2 + [L]^2`` | logK | -17.91 | (solid Hg2Cl2 dissolution)
Very negative dissolution logK (-17.91) implies sparing solubility of Hg2Cl2(s) and limited aqueous Hg+.
Hg+ + Chloroacetic acid at high I (2.775~3.225 M): logK 2.4 (ML), 4.4 (ML2).

PhHg+ + Hydroxide: logK 9.75 at 25 °C, I 0.1.
EtHg+ + Cl− (saline I=1): `[M]+[L]<=>[ML]` logK 5.32.
EtHg+ + Chloride: logK 5.32.
MeHg+ + Hydroxide: ΔS 58.6 (25 °C, I 0.1) for `[M] + [L] <=> [ML]`.
Tool results show ΔS for MeHg+ + OH− (25 °C, I 0.1) but do not prove absence of logK; query was limited (10 rows).
MeHg+ + OH− ΔS row shows T°C 25.
MeHg+ + OH− ΔS row shows I(M) 0.1.

paragraphs.text:
If you’d like, we can drill further into:
- Halide series (Cl−/Br−/I−) for Hg(II) and organomercury species under matched ionic strengths.
- Sulfur-donor ligands (thiolates) vs O/N donors for Hg(II).
- Full speciation networks at specific salinities and pH.
